content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street• Suite 6:?0 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Bruce Burton 
350 Sherwood Rd 
Willits, Calif 95490 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-231 
Dear Mr. Burton: 
January 12, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in City Council decisions regarding a property in 
which you have an interest in buying? 
CONCLUSION 
While the Act does not prohibit you from purchasing the property, the Act does prohibit you 
from taking part in any decisions by the City Council regarding the sale of the subject property 
because it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material financial effect on your 
personal finances. 
FACTS 
You are a City Council member for the City of Willits. Many years ago, Willits entered into 
a settlement agreement (approved by a federal district court) regarding an industrial property (the 
"Property") that was found to have environmental contamination. In the agreement, the owners 
agreed to certain clean-up and remediation parameters that they largely have completed. One of the 
stipulations in the agreement is that they would remove the buildings and foundations that remain. 
If the owners found a buyer for the property, however, they could determine the buyer's intended 
use and request that the City approve the sale while leaving the buildings and foundations intact. 
About six months ago the owner determined the clean-up project was sufficient to begin looking at 
selling the property. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. AJI 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-15-231 
Page No. 2 
Along with others, you have expressed interest in buying the property. To do so, the owners 
must present your group's intended use proposal to the city council, which would then approve or 
deny the sale. You state that you have not participated in decisions related to the Property since 
learning of the seller's intent to sell. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
As a City Council Member, you are a public official under the Act. (Section 87200.) 
A public official has a "financial interest" in a government decision, within the meaning of 
the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103.) Interests from which a disqualifying 
conflict of interest may arise include only the following: 
• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• An interest in a source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or 
more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) 
• An interest in a source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $460 or more within 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).) 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of his or her immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103) 
Because you have not identified a business in which you have a $2,000 or more interest, and 
you do not yet have a $2,000 or more investment in the Property, the only financial interest that 
applies here is an interest in your personal finances. 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) While the Property is explicitly involved in the City 
Council's decisions, your personal finances are not. Therefore, the foreseeability standard that 
applies is: "A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, 
if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 18701(b).) Because decisions that the City 
Council makes will inform whether or not you purchase the Property, those decisions are 
reasonably foreseeable to have an effect on your personal finances. The effect of the decisions will 
be material if the decisions will cause you or your immediate family to experience a "measurable 


File No. A-15-231 
Page No. 3 
financial gain or loss." Because the decisions will direct whether or not you will be able to purchase 
a multi-million dollar property, this standard is met. 
As such, you may not make, participate in making, or influence any decision regarding the 
Property. The Act does not preclude you from purchasing the Property, however. 
There are no facts to suggest that the legally required exception (Regulation 18705) or 
public generally exception (Regulation 18703) would apply. 
When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a city 
councilmember) has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: 
( 1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest 
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; 
(2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote 
on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.) 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HMR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Heather M. Rowan 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

While the Act does not prohibit you from purchasing the property, the Act does prohibit you 
from taking part in any decisions by the City Council regarding the sale of the subject property 
because it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material financial effect on your 
personal finances.
